INTRODUCTION TO BACHELOR THESIS The aim of this chapter is to provide a short overview of the paper, bring up the primary analysis of the problem discussed, identify an object and a subject of the research and the gap in business literature which caused the subject to be brought up and s tudied. 1.1 Literature review 1.1.1 Abstract With the occurrence and deepening of new global challenges, there is a change in stakeholders' expectations. Cancel Culture has become one of the characteristics of this change. Business Ethics, as a study whic h teaches how to govern stakeholder’s expectations, may be partially outdated if it does not take into account the recent changes.  1.1.2 Problem description Cancel Culture has become a permanent phenomenon in the West referring to cancelling people or co mpanies as cancelling a subscription on something - cancelling the social and economical support. Such a cancellation is a huge brand and employer image loss as well as financial one. Cancel culture implies risks for business, which is proven by many cases  worldwide. CC is an undefined and poorly studied phenomenon, as it is widely presented in the news and periodicals, though is not mentioned in the business related materials. The first signs of the phenomena started to penetrate business literature in 201 7, analysing the problem of corporate apologies to public and their effect on company’s image reconciliation. Later on, business ethics abstracts started the conversation about banning and boycotting companies and specific people. All mentioned without the  “Cancel Culture” part. Though,”cancelling” is easy to find in any periodical article describing cases of replacement caused by scandals or unethical work behaviour or public statements. Cancel Culture cases are caused by many reasons, which can be summari zed in one thought - people get cancelled by agreeing with the common norms, values and belief shift. This shift is mostly about being equal, having equal rights, opportunities and representation. Such an agenda was presented in business ethic articles fro m 2010 and earlier, having a significant rise from 2015, the year when the 17 sustainable goals were introduced which made these ideas tangible, desired and measurable. The deadline is set for 2030, though the society (some parts are more, some are less) a dapted to the goals faster than the due date for their implication, as it was needed and contained a real issue society wanted to overcome. This created a gap, as institutions were not as fast as society in terms of this shift, which created  Cancel Culture  - a stakeholder’s reaction to moral norms shift in a desire to reach the sustainable goals faster. Business ethics, as a study which tries to identify what is moral for business, have to identify its attitude for the new phenomena and to define it.  1.1.3 Review of Literature The prerequisites of Cancel Culture Cancel Culture is a new movement which is hard to ignore. It takes place in many countries around the world. In Russia it is known as “культура отмены”, which is proved by many occurings in  media . Cancel Culture, by the definition, is an old concept, which has always existed in people’s lives, as a form of social control in the form of rumors, whispers and censorship. Even if the phenomenon is associated with American activist movements, it s’ roots are claimed to be Chinese , as in such a highly competitive labor market with such a huge number of citizens it is so difficult to conduct a security or even a reputation check. So, the Human Flesh Search Engine was introduced, so your potential e mployer always knows about your past life. Today it has its rise in the US pumped by the BLT and #MeToo movements. Cancel Culture is also a part of a financial system, precisely the stock funds, as it is a form of the societal support to a company, while i nvesting is a form of financial support of the companies, or avoidance. The financial part of the Cancel Culture exists due to the ESG index introduction, which analyses the companies from the perspective of ecological, social and governance leadership, as  it showed its adaptability to the new moral norms and might be successful in the future . 1.1.4 Research method One of the objects of the research is to overcome the inconsistency with the ignorance of Cancel Culture in the business-related literature a nd its wide presence in non-scientific periodical one.  The goal is to conclude that Cancel Culture is actually present in the scientific literature, or at least its traits and border topics. It is crucial for the problem statement, as if Cancel Culture is  a temporary phenomenon, it will not lead to any changes in society, ethics and business and the study of business ethics do not have to deal with it, do not have to define it. On the other hand, if the phenomenon is present in periodical literature as well  as in academic one within a certain time and the study of business ethics is trying to deal with some ideas which lead to Cancel Culture occurrence, the research is actual and relevant. For making this statement, it was chosen to conduct a quantitative an alysis on the secondary data - all articles of Journal of Business Ethics from 2010 to 2020 by year, analysing the topics which takes the attention of business ethics community and which percent is contributed to Cancel Culture related topics, as well as t he topics which describes Cancel Culture without naming it. By the dynamic it is easy to conclude the following: 1) if Cancel Culture is a partial product of sustainable goals statement; 2) if Cancel Culture is discussed in the context of Business Ethics a nd may be the problem of it; 3) if Cancel Culture is a permanent problem which deserves the attention of the business community; Stating the importance of Cancel Culture within the modern business ethics discussion is not the only objective of the thesis,  as it is crucial to identify what risks it may bear and how it applies to the real business situations. For this reason, the qualitative analysis of a secondary data is conducted: a case study of companies both on Russian and the US market involved in canc elling cases. The identification of the common patterns may define certain traits of the phenomenon on the local market and the way it can differ with the cultural and institutional context.  1.2 Bachelor Thesis description 1.2.1  The goal of the Bachelor  Thesis To identify risks, opportunities and barriers for Business Ethics’ adaptation to changes in stakeholder expectations signaled by Cancel Culture on different markets.  1.2.2 Research object and subject The Research object chosen for the bachelor  thesis is: The analysis of US and Russian companies’ business ethics While the Research subject is: Its changes with the introduction of Cancel Culture 1.2.3 Research assumptions Cancel Culture should change Business Ethics. The extent to which Cancel Culture penetrates Business Ethics is not the same for all markets, due to cultural and institutional differences.   Cancel Culture has a negative impact on business in short-term, while having a positive one in long-term. 1.2.4  Research objectives To understand the roots of Cancel Culture To identify weather it is a substantial cultural change or a temporary one To define Cancel Culture place within the Business Ethics study To identify risks or ignoring or reacting to phenomen on To analyse the cultural differences affecting Cancel Culture acceptance  1.2.5 Characteristics of research methodology Research methodology differs in accordance to the assumption are to be tested: Cancel Culture should change Business Ethics:  A qua ntitative analysis of articles of Journal of Business Ethics within the 2010-2020 timeline. The extent to which Cancel Culture penetrates Business Ethics is not the same for all markets, due to cultural and institutional differences:  A qualitative analysi s of cases of Cancel Culture on two markets, state-level documents and corporate-level codes of ethics. Cancel Culture is a new basis for investment decisions:  A qualitative analysis ESG index implementation. 1.2.6 Characteristics of research data Secondary Articles by Journal of Business Ethics Codes of Conduct (Ethics) of companies operating on Russian and American market Global Agreements and State Documents on Sustainable challenges Annual reports and financial performances of canceled companie s Other Business, Culture and CSR related articles by business journals and news channels CHAPTER 2. CANCEL CULTURE AS A PERMANENT PROBLEM OF BUSINESS 2.1 Chapter’s important definitions  As all the new phenomena, Cancel Culture and other close and  relevant ideas need to be clarified, as the understanding of them may differ: Cancel culture  –  a way of behaving in a society or group, especially on social media, in which it is common to completely reject and stop supporting someone because they have said or done something that offends you.  Call out culture  –  a way of behaving in a society or g roup in which people are often criticized in public, for example on social media, for their words or actions, or asked to explain them. Psychological violence  -  an action aimed at hurting the integrity and dignity of another person. Bullying  -  the behaviour of a person who  hurts  or  f rightens someone smaller or less powerful, often forcing that person to do something they do not want to do. Activism  -  a doctrine or prac tice that emphasizes direct vigorous action especially in support of or opposition to one side of a controversial issue.  Points of activist intervention : Government and Laws (Protests, marches, civil disobedience, and other demonstrations, lobbying, ba llot measures, voter mobilization, media campaigns) Companies and Institutions (Protests, boycotts, strikes, and other demonstrations, lawsuits, creating alternative institutions, direct communication, regulatory bodies, shareholder voting, internal polici es, media campaigns) Individuals and Communities (Leafletting and other information distribution, direct communication, cooperatives and youth organizing groups, education, media campaigns) Organizational Factors (Recruitment, burnout prevention, strategy,  messaging, partnerships) Individual Actions (Career, giving choices, lifestyle changes, small acts) 2.1.2 Phenomena interconnection I characterize Cancel Culture as an end form of Call-Out culture - when the society calls out a person or a company, it  gives so to say a “first warning”, expressing disobedience to the entity’s actions and asks to give justification or change. If the change is impossible, or it does not take place, an entity gets cancelled by job dismissal, boykot, end of partnership, etc.  Call-Out culture as an activism can have violent and non-violent forms, while Cancel Culture is always violent. By violence is implied the psychological violence, which is mostly expressed by bullying and cyberbullying, as Cancel Culture is mostly a digit al phenomenon. It unites two levels of activism - Institutional and Community one. The start of an action begins on the individual level, though it will not become empowered until it reaches the commu nity levels. The action will stay just an expression until it reaches the institutional level and decision-making entities complete the canceling.  2.2 Global problems and Cancel Culture Today’s global challenges make society change their understanding of what is right and what is wrong on a personal level, and of course on a business one. The global challenges address pervasive issues like climate change, poverty, hunger, equitable econom ic development, and more. The 17 goals of Sustainability were set up in 2015 by the United Nations General Assembly and are intended to be achieved by the year 2030. The deepening of these goals started in 2015, but in 2021 reached the point when a lot of  companies and NGOs joined the common challenge for creating a better future.  Fig. 2 –  17 goals of Sustainability by United Nations (Source: UN) Cancel Culture is a social reaction to the new requirements set by SDGs on the one hand, while on the other  - it has a form which contradicts the new norms of ethics and is on the list on the problems to be addressed by global challenges.  These are the goals of Sustainability which can be addressed with Cancel Culture:   Goal 5 :  Achieve gender equality and e mpower all women and girls.  Target 5.1:  End all forms of discrimination against all women and girls everywhere. And while the proportion was slightly  higher for mothers, at 22%, some 19% of women with no children also said they had experience of this” - says PWC “Winning the fight for female talent” report . It can have not only ethical consequences, but the economical ones: “Companies in the top quartile for gender diversity on executive teams were 25 percent  more likely to have above-average profitability than companies in the fourth quartile”  - the conclusion of McKinsey&Co research of 2019.  Cancelling might be used to address this goal: to fight sexism and inequality in a working place by cancelling or ca lling out people who do not follow the ethical norms change. This is also an issue worth fighting for with measures of social justice, such as Cancel Culture. With many cases of different treatment,  opportunities and wages for male and female, native and migrant, disabled people or conventionally healthy ones. Though the other 2 goals (Goal 4 and 10) addr ess only social problems, while these 2 are directly connected with issues to be solved at a working place, as a global challenge to business. Though there are two Sustainable goals which makes the phenomenon questionable:  Goal 3:  Ensure healthy lives and  promote well-being for all at all ages;  Target 3.4:  By 2030, reduce by one third premature mortality from non-communicable diseases through prevention and treatment and promote mental health and well-being; Goal 16:  Promote peaceful and inclusive societie s for sustainable development, provide access to justice for all and build effective, accountable and inclusive institutions at all levels;  Target 16.1:  Significantly reduce all forms of violence and related death rates everywhere;  Target 16.2:  Promote the  rule of law at the national and international levels and ensure equal access to justice for all; Why Cancel Culture is a problem which needs to be addressed by these two Sustainable goals?  Because, it is usually realised in a form of psychological violence and cyberbullying which contradicts the values of the new moral norms for society and business set by global challenges. 2.3.2 Stakeholders analysis The identification of the stakeholders  of the Cancel Culture phenomenon is quite an important issue within the topic. First of all, it will enable us to understand the roots of the problem better, second of all, it will help to identify risks the CC has and the last, but not the least - to mak e a preliminary classification of CC cases by the stakeholder.  Fig. 5    –  Firm's stakeholders diagram To start with, it is important to analyse CC from the view of Stakeholder Theory and to give an estimation of which stakeholder groups are relevant in  the given context. Internal Stakeholders Let’s imagine an FMCG company X which was recently identified as racist by the common public due to the absence of people of color on their promotion materials. They need to somehow react to people trying to cance l them as they are losing their revenue and a good brand image.  The situation affects all the groups in a way the decision making is usually done: upside down.  Owners  set the strategic context of the situation, for example, with codex of ethics of the com pany. If it states that the firm supports inclusivity and equality, it shifts the ongoing reaction on the case. So, the  managerial group  of stakeholders choses how to answer the societal “attack”, so they will make the most of the people satisfied without  changing the company’s values and policies, saving brand image and revenues. And, logically, the group of  employees  are the once to bring the others’ decisions into life. So, I would conclude that the degree to which Cancel Culture plays its role is depend ent on the level of responsibilities, going down with the entity’s step in companies’ hierarchy. It also works in situations when the negativity recipient is a part of the company: the lower the position, the lower the risks. Which is why it is a primary  g oal of owners to estimate risks of a certain position their company is holding within the societal new norms and expectations.  External stakeholders  Taking the same company X from the previous paragraph as an example, I would like to structure the exte rnal stakeholders the same way, beginning with the most influential in terms of the power of canceling a business. The criteria for the structuring are quite simple: the damage an entity can cause to a brand image, the relevance of an entity to a considere d firm, and its financial power. Customers is the group which has the most power due to its direct relevance to the company’s sales. And if decided unethical the company can suffer from great financial losses. Shareholders is the group which has also a great power to affect the financials of the company. So if there will be any scandal or negativity towards the company, shareholders can interpret the situation as frightening to their capital and start selling sh ares so their price will greatly go down. Talking about the shareholders who have a big share per cent, the situation can also have a negative impact on the reputation of such people or companies. Society can have a very big impact reputation wise, though  not all of the people in a society are the customers of this concrete firm, so companies don’t bear the direct financial losses.  Creditors play a bit of a similar role as the shareholders, as they have a big power to affect a company's financials, though  have a high possibility of substitution and the negative consequences can be not bigger than a closure of the future credit lines, which can be managed.  Suppliers play a smaller role, due to their high possibility of substitution. It is important to unde rline that the conclusion covers only the FMCG sector, as was mentioned, though the situation changes in the context of other industries. SpaceX suppliers, for instance, play a crucial role and have a high importance, but it is quite unlikely that these tw o entities would cancel each other as they are mutually dependent.  Government plays almost no role in canceling as it follows the rules of law, though the culture of canceling goes far beyond the laws and makes its own justice. Government, in this situati on, can have a public point of view, which can support or contradict with the public one, but it can cause no great influence. Fig. 6    –  The power of cancelling by a stakeholder (Made by author) In the context of Cancel Culture, I believe, there should be three groups of stakeholders, not 9: Internal Stakeholders  - people who work for the company involved in the CC case. It is important to group them separately as this case is an issue to solve f or the HR department of a company, with their particular practises and functions. Canceling may lower the morale down the employees to a great extent, which is a managerial problem to be studied closer. Financial Stakeholders -  As it can be noticed from t he previous stakeholder’s analysis, at least 3 groups directly influence financial performance of a company. Nowadays, the ethical situation within a company plays a crucial role in its financial stability and investors know that. One of the major proofs i s the ESG index. ESG takes into account the Ecological, Social and Governance state of a corporation, analysing it with more than 400 criteria. The index is also applicable to the Russian market, as there are existing financial advisory firms calculating E SG . Activists Stakeholders -  These are media, NGOs and separate activists who represent and shape societies' views. This is the most interesting group, as its behaviour changes from culture to culture, while the others groups remain predictable. Activist s stakeholders decide on which case to react and in which circumstances, they are the X factor in the CC case management equation.   2.4 Cancel Culture and the responsibility of business To manage the CC crisis or even prevent it, it would be helpful to  understand what the stakeholders want from firms, what behaviour or actions can be taken as “enough” to arise a positive feedback from a society. Fig. 7    –  Carrol's Pyramid of Corporate Responsibility (Source: A. Carrol)  Carrol’s pyramid helps us to u nderstand the levels of expectations from society on how the business should operate, though it represents a little bit outdated concept of the way responsibilities are perceived. Nowadays, the concept of three pillars of Corporate Sustainability is more r elevant in terms of analysing the CSR of business. The following table has all the pillars allocated in the sequence by importance increase: 1. Environmental This pillar usually is the central one in the context of responsibilities. Most of the biggest companies have shifted their production to the zero-vaste one, trying to find new ways to decrease the footprint they leave. There are some stakeholders trying t o raise the awareness in society to call out companies, who do not follow this new agenda. For example, Greenpeace trying to call out Nestle and other big producers for irresponsible packaging . 2. Social Social pillar goes second and underlines the resp onsibility of business for being ethical to its employees, the community it operates in and other stakeholders. Here is where the most CC  cases happen, as companies are being unethical to stakeholders through its products or communications, as well as bein g unethical to employees, providing unsafe or discriminating circumstances. 3. Economic This pillar is quite simple in interpretation, as with this concept, business is sustainable if it is profitable. In the context of CC, there are two terms which are  interesting about this pillar: compliance and corporate governance. The compliance variable is about going along with rules under which business operates and some of them are environmental and social. The CG variable is about meeting aligning the managemen t, owners, shareholders and stakeholders vision together.  2.5 Cancel Culture risks identified    To conclude on what potential threat Cancel Culture can have it is crucial to compare and contrast the new expectations set by Global challenges with the type s of stakeholders identified by my previous research. For this purpose, the Cancel Culture risks matrix is made: Table 1.  Cancel Culture risks matrix Internal Stakeholders Financial Stakeholders Activists Stakeholders  Environmental Responsibility An employee of a company which is not following the new Global challenges 1) can suffer from a low morale, if company does not share the values of staff; 2) can suffer from some negative health impact, as meeting new requirements is also reducing risks at  a  workplace, particularly in cases of specific industries; Due to the sustainable investing trend in business and education, people do not want to invest in ecological pollution; “Ecological” is not only about packaging, but also about the content, which i s directly relevant to the quality of a product, which is seen on sales and incomes of the shareholders.  The goal of activist stakeholders is to make business be more responsible socially and environmentally and protect victims of such cases, maintaining  social justice. In terms of business, this creates a huge brand image decline followed by financial  threads. Activist stakeholders have a reactional way of working.  Social Responsibility This is the most important part for an employee and a job seeker to pay their attention to a certain company. In case of poor working conditions, harassment or abuse cases, etc. This issue may decrease the number of quality employees and provoke high turno ver. Social responsibility is a sign of a trustworthy business, which will grow with the best diverse employee presence. It will be protected from lawsuits and other causes which can affect the dividends of investors, so it can attract more finances, from  the side of employees as well. From the matrix, it is seen that Cancel Culture has two major threads for business:  Risk of Employer Brand jeopardising  As the example of the US discussed, there is a cost of poor brand image calculated: for a company with 10,000 employees could be as  much as $7.6 million in additional wages. Based on  the average US salary being $47,230, assumed annual turnover of 16.4%, and a minimum 10% per cent pay rise . The numbers leads to the next issue Cancel Culture causes: Risk of Investments loss Nowadays,  companies who are not responsible enough or who operate in the sphere which pollutes environment a lot will keep losing its value early . Risk of Consumer Brand jeopardising Imagine a case, then a company turned to be quite careless to the problem of ecology, while specializing on selling goods, from groceries to soaps. The consumers of such a company will start questioning the harmlessness of the products bought, which will d rop sales and consumer brand image. The same case can happen to a company, promoting its family values which turned to use child labor of one of their production, a female value creating company will participate in a harrasemnet lawsuit. All the cases ment ioned are underlying that Cancel Culture can also be going from customers - boycotting and stopping buying from it . 2.3 Global problems and a change in stakeholder’s expectations So, new global challenges raised the problems of equality in all its sense s and preservation of ecology, which changed the expectations of companies’ stakeholders. To analyse where, how and on what levels these changes happened, I analyse the business ethics related publications within the last 10 years. By this, I state that th e values have changed, though, I go further to conclude which stakeholders’ values have changed and how it affects the organizations.  2.3.1 Cancel Culture as a point of academic research To prove the assumption that Cancel Culture has to change Business Ethics, the research has to prove that Cancel Culture is a substantial phenomenon and is linked to modern business agenda. To prove that, I have chosen a 10-year period to study if the root s of the phenomenon can be traced within the timeline. I have marked the articles that are relevant to the modern Cancel Culture agenda and are connected to it in some way.  Which articles are CC-related and which are not? The previous analysis is taken  as a research basis. So, the topics may include articles relevant to the 1) stakeholders identified; 2) risks CC has; 3) reasons caused cancelling; 4) opportunities the new challenges have;  Table 2.  - Research reasoning examples Groups Relevant  Non-relev ant Stakeholders You Support Diversity, But Are You Ethical? Evidence Using Stock Portfolio Performance Advancing Ethics in Public Organizations: The Impact of an Ethics Program on Employees’ Perceptions and Behaviors in a Regional Council Stakeholders-Relevant The article studie s the topic of ethical climate on turnovers. This article is quite informative, as it brings up several points of discussion: 1) stakeholder interests, in particular, internal stakeholders, who want to work at an ethical firm where his or her inputs are va lued and the opportunities are distributed equally; 2) risks, as it gives a talk about the turnover, which is quite a risky in terms of financial performance side effect of being unethical; 3) Opportunities, as the  articles brings up the idea that being et hical will have a positive impact on the employees morale, will decrease turnover and the efficiency.  Stakeholders-Non-Relevant The article does not interest us as it studies CSR in general, with no risks, opportunities, etc. The article studies the sit uation in family firms, nothing more. So, it can not potentially lead to cancelling or calling out. Risks-Relevant The article is directly related to Cancel Culture, as it gives a talk on sandals within the firm and the way they are to be managed. Information disclosures usually act like a small fire to start a scandal which leads to cancellation or calling out. That i s why the article is important in Cancel Culture discussion. Risks-Non-Relevant The article gives a talk on how to introduce CSR practises within a firm without increasing different types of risks. This is a manual for companies which are not ethical eth ough and want to bend more to this direction. There are quite different kinds of risks mentioned, that is why the article is not partially or directly important.  Reasons-Relevant T he article analyses the performance of a company with different degrees o f diversity to conclude that gender diversified companies are more efficient and have better results. Such an idea represents an opportunity for other companies to diversify their employees' genders and backgrounds to create the most efficient tems. Reaso ns-Non-Relevant Even though there is a talk about inequality, and to be precise an income inequality, this is not the inequality which can be relevant in Cancel Culture. The gender income inequality, the racial income inequality may be the topic, the reaso n for canceling to break out within a company.  Though the research is about the inequality within the society which the natural development of some institutions caused. You can not cancel institutions.  Opportunities-Relevant There is a bias within a man agerial  society that having a female board of directors makes a company more concervative and less risk-tolerant, which affects the stock performance and many other spheres of the company’s interest. The bias is quite dangerous in terms of its ability to  cause cancellation in a working place. CHAPTER 5. ESG AS AN ANSWER TO A BUSINESS ETHICS GAP  In the previous chapters it was stated and discussed the roots, the application and the importance of the problem of Cancel Culture within the Business Ethics. The problem is not identified institutionally, so each company decides for itself on how to reac t and how to prevent it, which is the gap this research is trying to cover. Though, there is one phenomenon which underlies the permanent character of Cancel Culture, emphasises the rise of cancelling in the future and answers the companies’ dilema: to rea ct or to be absent.  5.1 Chapter's important definitions Compliance management  - the process by which managers plan, organize, control, and lead activities that ensure compliance with laws and standards. ESG index  - stands for Environmental, Social,  and Governance. Investors are increasingly applying these non-financial factors as part of their analysis process to identify material risks and growth opportunities. 5.2 Emotional cancelling Stock trading can be addressed as a cancelling agent too, as t he investors give their financial support to the companies they believe are worthy. By selling or avoiding stock purchase, potential investors choose not to allocate their financials to these companies. For business it is quite a challenge, as these compan ies will have a harder time raising funds and harder time to do business in general. An example of such responsible investing is the automobile industry. Taking Teska as an example, it is clearly seen that even such quality cars with its branding and Elon  Musk as a CEO probably does not worth the $800 billion market cap, as it has now. It can be  explained by the superior mission of Tesla, as people think that electric vehicles are the future and they are probably correct.  Moreover, Tesla is saving the envi ronment which is a problem that currently needs to be addressed. Ford and General Motors  both have introduced their electric Mustang Mach-E (In april 2021) and electric delivery vans (in January 20210 and made their stocks to bump up quite despite the fac t that these cars are quite unlikely to be a part of the common car flow within the next decade. Fig. 9  - The stock performance of F after launching Mustang Mash-E (Source: NASDAQ) Fig. 10  - The stock performance of GE after launching an eVan  (Source: NASDAQ) 5.3 Rational cancelling The other approach to Cancel Culture within stock trading is about its adaptation capacity. The time changes as well as stakeholder expectations to the way bu siness has to be conducted. That is why there is a natural selection for the firms splitting them into two categories: the ones who adopt to new moral norms and the ones who do not. It is a simple measure and it can show not only the adaptation to some soc ietal changes, though to technological ones. This guarantees the investors that the companies they put their money in are strong enough and will last.  Taking as an example Tesco the English retailer company, which continually analyses the customer data bu ilding patterns and makes customized offers and changes within the chains.Tesco also developed their own online platform, which enabled Tesco to shift their business model and create a broader range of services. This is an example of a quite adoptive compa ny in terms of the technology and customer orientation. How about their orientation to the new societal challenges?  Fig. 11  - The stock performance of TSCO  (Source: LSE) Its stock price is quite stable within the last 3 years and even after the coronavirus crisis as a retailer it could stabilize its positions quite quickly to the level it was previously set . It characterises the company as a reliable one, the one the inve stors could put their funds in and won’t lose it.  What about the policies of Tesco? Do they support the changed moral standards or not?  It was important to say that there were no scandals which Tesco participated in which are connected with the common is sues Cancel Culture is about. Tesco have quite big passages on  code of ethics and have spare codes on Diversity and Inclusion , Bullying and Harassment , Ecology  and a whole program on taking action where it is seen how the company is improving its indexe s through time.  5.3 ESG index in investing  ESG  is the environmental, social, and governance aspects to take a look at analysing the possible success of a company's stocks. The ESG was introduced not long ago to compare to stock trading. And it came to  life as a reaction to people, investors, who acted not always rationally, but in accordance with their beliefs and principles. ESG investing is a form of Cancel Culture because the way such people allocate capital is based on their idea of the future, whic h will be honest, moral, green and tolerant. how they believe the world should work and not necessarily on how the world actually does work. They are trying to cancel these companies and industries by not investing.  To fully lead the cause and the develop ment of ESG index as it is now, It will be tracked to its early forms: The forming of ESG started in 1990 with the Domini Social 400 Index   which is the data on capitalization weighted index of 400 US securities of eligible companies based on considerations  of ESG performance, sector alignment and size representation that provides exposure to companies with outstanding ESG ratings and excludes companies whose products have negative social or environmental impacts. 11 years after, in 2001 the KLD Large-Mid Ca p Social Index of the US was introduced. It still has 400 companies in its consideration set, comparing their performance within the market and the sector. 2007 was the year of 3 ESG-related occurings: 1) MSCI USA ESG Leaders Index, which was a relaunch of  a 2001 LD Large-Mid Cap Social Index; 2) Sustainability Index (GSI), which was an index for the countries, not the companies on the addoptivity to sustainable norms; 3) The issued IPO by MSCI by Morgan Stanley taking with it the global index benchmarks; I n 2009 MSCI Global Environment Index was introduced - the index takes into account a set of companies whose main products are connected with Alternative Energy, Sustainable Water, Green Building, Pollution Prevention or Clean Technology .  In 2011 it is MS CI World SRI Index of companies stocks across 23 Developed Markets and excludes the companies who had bad ecological or social impact. This was the first Socially Responsible Investment (SRI) guide benchmarking companies for its ESG implications .  In 2014  MSCI Global Low Carbon Indexes were introduced aimed at benchmarking for socially responsible investors particularly on the low carbon economy and its potential risks . The same one was introduced on fossil fuels.  In 2016 MSCI declines from the ecologica l agenda into the social one, as it introduces World Women Leadership index , which is a form of MSCI World Index though takes into account the firms which state gender diversity as its key priorities in ESG. In 2019 Climate Change Indexes are introduced,  which is currently an end form of climate-focused ESG. It consists of 3 principles of analysis: “Shift from brown to green” , “Broad & diversified” and “Multifunctional”. Summing up, the indexes has been in development for ages, since 1990 evolving and dif ferentiating. Currently it is not one, but a set of different indexes which emphasise different ideas of equality and green economy. This underlines the long history of Cancel Culture and the importance of investors, as a stakeholder group.   CHAPTER 6. CONCLUSIVE SUMMARY  Cancel Culture is a part of the modal dilemma which Business Ethics has to define and answer, as the phenomenon is the cause of new moral norms prompted by the introduction of 17 sustainable goals. Moreover, within sustaina bility it, on the one hand, advocates several of its goals, though contradicts the one on global mental health, as it represents a form of activism using negative actions, such as bullying.  Cancel Culture happens when the community activist meets the inst itutional activism, i.e. scandals cause resignation, as a risk management measure. There are three groups of stakeholders in cancelling a company or a person: internal (the employees who can suffer due to having the moral bars which are not compliant with  its employer ones, increasing turnover and decreasing morale), financial (the investors, lenders and the other external entities who affect company’s revenues) and activism ones (the active group who may be responsible for cancelling due to the fact that i t is the force which changes moral norms through time, as well as laws). This creates the related threads of employer brand loss, investments loss and brand image loss.  The most evident scandal cases happen due to the three groups of reasons by three pill ars of Corporate Responsibility - Ecological, Social and Governance. Governance part is an earth-old basis for compliance and law following, so it is not within the consideration. Though, Ecological and Social aspects are 6 years old (from the year Sustain able goals were introduced).  Ecological cases never result in cancellation due to impossibility to identify the particular person who was responsible. The most common topic for cancelling is gender-related. Most of it happens in the US, as there were no non-cancelling cases, though in  Russia it provokes many scandals, though not always with a consequence. Russian approach is quite a different one to the American due to cultural differences and the development of institutions.  Even if Ethics of business has not defined Cancel Culture yet, it is a common practive within stock trading, as it is simply about the financial support of some companies over the othes due to emotional reasons, such as the willingness to be a part of somethng good or rational resons, as firms who adapt to new moral norms are likely to ada pt to the other rapidly changing environment. Цели:
1. Развитие культуры инклюзивности в России.
2. Сокращение случаев сексуального харассмента в российских компаниях.

Задачи:
1. Организация кампаний по повышению осведомленности населения о проблемах инклюзивности и борьбе с сексуальным харассментом.
2. Разработка и реализация программ поддержки жертв сексуального харассмента.
3. Создание условий для открытой и безопасной работы женщин в профессиональном сообществе.
4. Обеспечение доступа людей с ограниченными возможностями к образовательным ресурсам и социальной защите.
5. Расширение прав и свобод граждан, включая права на равноправие и свободу от дискриминации.
6. Повышение ответственности работодателей за создание благоприятных условий труда и предотвращение сексуального харассмента.
7. Обучение и консультирование специалистов по вопросам инклюзивности и борьбы с сексуальным харассментом.
8. Активизация общественного движения за развитие культуры инклюзивности и борьбу с сексуальным харассментом.
